sentences.text: I couldn't walk.
I could hardly crawl.
Yes, it did.
I wouldn't have never made the march, no way.
My two buddies who did the march, they never made it.
No way.
It was just not in the cards.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Matter of fact, the doctor who counseled me on that lives in Paris today.
Rolling slate.
Today's date, January 20, 1995.
The survivor is Louis Posner, the interviewer, Judy Flesh, the city, Los Angeles, the country, United States of America.
Language will be English.
Louis-- L-O-U-I-S, Posner-- P-O-S-N-E-R.
April 9, 1926.
Berlin, Germany.
Well, I guess Berlin, everybody knows Berlin.
It's a big city.
At my birth, I suppose they had about four million inhabitants.
I was born in 1926 from a Polish mother and a German father.
My father met my mother in Poland in the first World War.
Outside of Lodz, there were about a unit of 11 soldiers.
And on an evening, Friday evening, which is the Sabbath, the father-- or the future father-in-law-- saw one soldier with a knapsack in the back.
And he saw a tallit right in the knapsack.
Jewish soldiers took a tallit with them into the front lines.
And he told my future mother, go and take the soldier in on [NON-ENGLISH], or Sabbath Friday night.
No Jewish soldier should stay outside.
That's the way they met.
And as I understand later from my dad's talking, they spent a week together every day while they were stationed there and finally decided to get married.
As I recall, I think, my father saying that for my mother, it was a  way out.
Interesting enough, they were very poor in Poland.
They lived in one room with seven people and no beds.
They slept on the ground.
So for my mother, who was a devout communist and a Bolshevik, it was a way out to go to the west.
They got married.
My father took my mother to Berlin.
They got married in Berlin.
He went back to the front lines.
And she stayed with my aunt, my father's sister.

And as I understand, again, from my father's talking, while she was all by herself, she spoke only Polish and Yiddish, hated the Germans, never learned German, only spoke Yiddish at home, and associated herself with-- the Spartans, in those days, were the Communist party.
Karl Liebknecht, Rosa Luxembourg marched on the streets, shouted against the Kaiser, against the war and all kinds of activities.
In 1918, when my father came back, who was a conservative Democrat, he continued his profession, which was a CPA.
He went to the University of Berlin and made some kind of a degree.
He became a lawyer, a tax lawyer-- what we call today a tax lawyer.
And in the meantime, life went on in 1923 with the famous Putsch of Hitler.
My mother was in the streets yelling, kill the Nazis.
Kill everybody.
Of course, as a Jewess, that was not the thing to do.
The problem arising, that the two entities, my father and my mother, didn't get along emotionally.
So they were fighting every day, like dog and cat.
By 1926, I was born, the little man, which, in a way, was a blessing to both of them, because it gave a little peace in the house.
At that time, my father was very, very busy from 8 o'clock in the morning till 1 o'clock at night, creating new clients, creating money for the household.
And interesting enough, even that my mother was a Bolshevik, she still had a kosher household, which I could, could never understand.
The two things really don't go together.
But I-- listen, I never tried to understand.
And there were moments of peace in the house, especially on Friday night, which was very nice.
I don't know much about my mother's family.
The only one I really knew was my mother, who died very early.
In my father's family, I know more.
They were, for 10 generations, in Germany, coming originally out of the province of Poznan, where all the Poznans come from.
The migration went about 1653, around that time, toward France, Holland, Austria, and Germany.
My father's family went to Silesia, which the city is today is a Polish name, Breslau.
And in Breslau, they were calling themselves Pozener, which means a people from Posen or Poznan.
At that time, all the Posners-- the original men was a rabbi, as I understand, a teacher.
And they belonged to the district of Posnanski.
Those were the barons in Poland who owned all the district.
And this is where the name Posner comes from.
So the whole family stems from, really, Breslau-- Father, with three boys and three girls.
And, as I understand, my grandfather, who I've never met, had a hardware store, a small hardware store.
The son was supposed to take it over, but unfortunately, passed away.
Was shot the first day on the, on the front lines in 1914.
The-- my father went to the front line four years.
Excuse me.
And my uncle never went to the front lines.
He was a technical artist.
And when the officer inducted him, asked him for his profession, he said, I'm a painter and a technical artist.
He said, can you draw maps?
And my uncle said, yes.
He says, come on to the back line.
All he did was draw war maps.
Matter of fact, they still have his name on in the archives.
And got married.
My uncle, Siegfried, got married.
Left Germany in 1926 because he got a divorce.
In those days, you didn't get a divorce over there.
Went to Prague and lived there.
And my-- the three girls, one of them went to Berlin, became a concert pianist in the '20s for four years with the Berlin Philharmonic.
And my father and his sister, they were very nice together.
They got along very well.
We lived in Berlin.
The only thing I can remember is, maybe, the Friday nights, which were very secure in a nice home.
Everything was set for the Sabbath evening.
And the lights were lit.
It was really a nice Jewish togetherness.
My father was home.
And Saturday, you went to the synagogue, to the shul.
It was just a little, tiny room they made into a shul.
And a little guy doesn't really know much in those years.
I think my first experiences were when I was about five years old.
I started to roam the streets.
And the reason for it, Father and Mother, when they were home, they were fighting.
My mother had heavy asthma.
And she was a forerunner of the feminist movement.
She smoked like 40 cigarettes a day and 10 at night, extremely high strung.
So I got hit about three, four times a day.
Once I knew why, and the other three times I never knew why.
And with that, I went down and roamed the streets, got into all kinds of troubles and all kinds of things.
And, of course, as I understood later, in those days already the Nazi movement was strong.
You could hear the SR marching through the streets.
And I looked as golden blond with blue eyes, like a little German boy.
Nobody took me for a Jew.
So I took advantage of it, inadvertently.
When I was six years old, I started my first day in school.
And, again, according to the wishes of my mother, it was, was called [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] is a free school.
You don't learn ABC.
You learn citizenship.
It was really, I think, promoted and sponsored by the communistic movement in Berlin.
That's what it really was.
Matter of fact, in the [GERMAN], one of those streets.
We lived in the northern part, which is about-- anybody who knows Berlin, it's about five blocks away from Alexanderplatz, that close to the business center of the city.
It was called [GERMAN], N-54, North 54.
My first year in school was terrific.
The teacher loved me.
He loved my mother, apparently.
And I had a good time.
Unfortunately, about a month before going into the second grade, my mother had a nicotine poisoning.
Apparently she hacked while she had asthma, and a little tiny opening in the lungs created the poisoning.
They put her in a hospital, of course [GERMAN].
And they closed it.
Apparently, somehow it correlated.
I don't know what.
Two days later, she had a heart attack.
Another day later, she had pneumonia.
And now comes the interesting part, because in many respects, Hitler was shortly before the rise.
This is 1933, now.
And, of course, a little guy still does know him from Adam.
And the father doesn't tell him anything because, you know, he's too young.
I'll tell you later, this was a famous expression.
And on a Saturday afternoon, we went visiting her.
And she laid in bed.
She looked at my dad and says, take the boy.
Get out of the country.
Bad times will come.
The famous expression, which comes later up and up and up again, my father says, why?
I am a German.
I was a soldier.
I'm decorated.
He had the Iron Cross first class, Iron Cross second class, a cross of honor, the, the Purple Heart.
Why?
She didn't say anything.
A 1 o'clock in the morning, while she was asleep, she passed on.
And it's also interesting that the German Jew was so conservative, so assimilated in the life of Germany, per se, that, I think in America you call it, he didn't see the forest for so many trees.
I remember the day when she was buried.
They took me to the morgue.
And I'm like close to seven years old, about two weeks away.
And the rabbi comes.
And, of course, it was a typical Jewish burial-- just in a sheet, no clothing, no coffin, just on a slab, drab lights.
And the rabbi comes in and looks at my father and says, are you crazy?
This is a seven-year-old.
Get him up in the office.
It is to be noted that my father never saw the grave.
He went there.
They put her down.
And he ordered a, a stone.
But, according to the Jewish rights, because he remarried six months later, you never visit the former wife's grave.
This is what I was told.
How much correct this is, I have no idea.
And I never saw it.
So six months later-- in the meantime, for six months I stayed with a family who were his clients, very nice people.
But I had to change school because Hitler came to power.
The free school was dismantled.
And I didn't learn.
For some reason, when the mother died and my father came on Sunday and said she passed away, I cried, I mean, apparently, for two days, and I never cried again.
Psychological, possibly.
I don't know.
And while staying with that family for six months-- it's a very famous name, Alper.
Probably, you don't know.
Gita Alper was a toast of the town in the earlier movie days.
She's buried in Westwood in a mausoleum, right above Marilyn Monroe.
Those were the family.
That was the family.
And I didn't learn in school.
I was dreaming.
While the teacher was teaching, I looked out the window and looked at the birds.
And I picked fights.
I became rowdy, a brawler.
And I was a pretty husky little guy.
So six months later, my father married again.
It was a conventional marriage.
He needed a woman in the house, needed a woman for the son.
And it was a spinster.
She'd never had any children, also highly nervous, extremely religious, didn't like children.
But it was a good marriage.
What can I tell you?
So there's the little guy who didn't like his first mother, who didn't like his second mother.
Neither one of them liked him.
And I grew up in that kind of environment.
My father, I rarely saw because he worked always.
When he came home, I was already in bed.
And so I was pretty much on my own.
And very good in sports.
I played ice hockey.
I played soccer.
I did inside gymnastics.
I was just a little, tiny powerhouse.
Matter of fact, when I boxed for the Maccabi when I was about 12 years old, I weighed like 138 pounds.
And I was five foot two.
I looked like Popeye.
And yeah, I was a husky little guy.
And you said the wrong word to me and I popped away.
And I got into all kinds of problems.
The teachers would call my father if he was home or would call my mother, and all kinds of headaches.
But politically, as a youngster, you don't know what's going.
You know, today, people tell me, oh, I remember when I was five years old.
They were-- this is nonsense.
When you're seven and eight years old, all you're interested in is play around, do dumb things, get into mischief.
You don't know what's going on.
But as you grow up in the city where there's so much noise-- and Berlin was a noisy city, a big city.
The Olympics were on, so you could see.
By that time already, the movement started to harass the Jews.

You had benches in the parks which said 'Jews, Negroes, and dogs not allowed.' You had the public swimming pools, 'Jews, Negroes, and Dogs not allowed.' Or you had public bathrooms, and it said, 'For Aryans only,' which in my studies later on, is a joke, because an Aryan-- a German is never an Aryan.
The true Aryans are the Hindus.
Where he ever got the name from, who knows?
But you could see the dark clouds coming on.
And even as a youngster, you began to notice.
Because the boys you played with when you were five, six, and seven, they wore Hitler uniforms.
They wore [GERMAN] uniforms.
And they looked at you and says, we can't talk to you.
You're a Jew.
And it was interesting that, whenever you called me a dirty Jew, I didn't react.
That went into left and went out right.
Who cares?
Only when you hit a friend of mine or insulted him, I would get angry.
I never found out why, and I was never interested in why.
But this was a turning point, because in 1937, the springtime-- I have to go proceed, because in 1935 we had to leave the public schools.
I went to public schools till then.
We all had to go into Jewish private schools.
And the teacher, who hated my guts, was an anti-Semite.
At one time, he hit me.
We got corporal punishments.
It was bamboo sticks.
And he hit me so hard that I had welts on my back.
And, inadvertently, my mother, when I got undressed in my room, saw it, didn't say anything.
At night, my father came in my room and says, come on out of bed.
I says, yeah?
And he looked at my back.
He says, why?
I said, I have no idea why.
I did something wrong, but, you know, he hit me more than that.
Anyhow, my dad, the next day, went to the school and told the principal, you know, it's all right to hit him.
But don't kill him.
That's not necessary.
As it turned out, my father had much power in the politics of things.
How, why, I don't know.
The teacher got dismissed and had to go to another school.
So the rights were still there.
From the Wannsee Conference and all that, I had no idea.
This is all something I found out later.
So by 1937, I'm walking home from school-- about a half an hour away.
And I see a little tiny fellow who was a friend of mine in my same class.
And he got harassed by a Hitler boy.
And matter of fact, his family name was Groft.
Don't ask me why I remember.
So as he was hassling him and pushing him, I went in between.
And I says, why don't you try me?
And I tried.
And I pummeled away to the point, when I came home-- the boy fell to the ground.
I took my little friend, and we went home.
In the evening, there was a knock on the door.
And my mother didn't open the door, already frightened.
My father opened the door, and she introduced herself.
And most professional people had their business part in the apartment when you lived in an apartment.
So he took her in his office.
And they got to talking and talking.
And, after about a half an hour, she left.
And my dad came into my room and got me out of bed.
I went into his office.
And said to me, do you got any idea what you did today?
I says, yeah, I kicked the out of a Hitler boy.
He says, yeah, but you did more than that.
You broke his nose.
He got two loose teeth.
He got cracked ribs.
What did you do to him?
I says, not much, just boxed him a little.
So my father said, you're a danger to us.
You're a danger to yourself.
Tomorrow morning, I'm going to put you into a boy's home about an hour away from the city.
And I must also tell you that my dad was a highly disciplinarian man, a highly conformed man.
At sharp 8 o'clock, he left.
A taxi was waiting for him to take him to the first job every morning.
In the dining room, you did not sit in his chair.
He sat in his chair.
When you started eating, you didn't talk.
You only talked when he stated smoking his cigarette after the dinner was finished.
And every morning, when you went to school, the shoes had to be polished.
The nails had to be clean.
You had to be combed and groomed, and you were looked all over.
So it was a highly typical proud German disciplinarian person.
He was just a German.
When I do talk to youngsters, I always tell them that being Jewish is my faith.
But I'm also a German.
And I grew up in that system, in the disciplinarian system, in that kind of a cleanliness, or whatever you want to call it.
And you have to separate the two entities.
You can't get out of it.
Even today, I am highly organized in my home.
It looks just like when I was a youngster.
That's just one of those things.
And it's contagious.
My son is the same way.
And he's born in America.
So the next morning, anyway, my mother said goodbye to me.
And my father went with the metropolitan.
We went out.
And I got introduced to the people in Zehlendorf.
It's a very nice area in Berlin.
And they had about five other boys who stayed there permanently, and about three or four girls.
And then they had, every four weeks, from the Jewish community, they had about 20 youngsters for four weeks who would stay there and recuperate because there was no food at home.
They came out of poorer section out of Berlin.
And the Jewish community paid for it.
So my dad said goodbye.
And he says, every Monday stop by at the house and see if there's anything new or there's nothing new.
And I took the subway for about an hour every morning to go to school.
I got up at quarter to 6:00 in the morning.
I left at about quarter to 7:00, arrived about a quarter to 8:00 in the city, and walked for 50 minutes to go to school.
And then, by two o'clock, when we went out of school, the same thing, went back again.
Homework, I never did.
I was as lazy as can come.
My only conning, I think, was that I had a photographic memory.
I copied everything.
I never knew how to do anything.
I just copied.
So whatever was on the blackboard, I copied.
And when the examines came or the tests came, I just copied the whole thing.
So they could never flunk me.
And the teachers hated me, which is one of those things.
I was a con artist and only excelled in sports.
I had some cousins who were heavyweight fighters for the Maccabi.
That's the way I got into the Maccabi Accoua.
And life went on.
The Nazis got bigger.
Anti-Semitism went bigger.
Once the Olympic was gone, everything really bloomed up.
All kinds of things were forbidden.
And by 1938, I went to school in the morning not knowing what Kristallnacht meant.
This is all something you learn later.
By this time, I went every Monday home.
And my mother gave me a bag of cookies after four hours.
And I went back to the, to the home.
But I wasn't interested in politics or anything like that.
I was interested in girls.
And I was interested in other boys, how to beat them in all this, sports.
And by 1938, I got up in the morning, just like usual, got my pack, went to the sub.
And I always stayed in the back car, so if I did have some homework and I wanted to do some, I could do it there.
And as the metro leaves the station, there's one man who has a newspaper there.
And he's reading in German.
And he's really saying, oh, man, they're burning the synagogues.
They're putting the Jews in camps.
And I'm listening to all this.
And he's raving and ranting to the people who come into the wagons.
And by about half an hour in the ride, I got a little scared.
I said, gee, this is not right.
What's going on?
So I stepped out, and I walked the rest of the way.
We were used to walking, never mind cars.
And I walked by synagogues who were burning, who were destroyed.
I saw people being herded in corners, men.
Matter of fact, in one synagogue I stopped.
And I asked him, what is going on?
He says, are you crazy?
Go on your way or they're going to pick you up, too.
I'm still not understanding what's going on.
I see the fire department.
They're looking the other way, or they're hosing the houses next to it.
The police department looks the other way.
And if at all times I thought that only the low-class citizen-- socially, you know, not high-- would do things like that or the thugs or the hoods, forget it.
Well-to-do people, well-dressed people were cheering by the fires, by the burning, by the arresting.
The shops were destroyed.
I mean, glasses were destroyed.
I don't know if there was any looting going on, but you saw the glass on the streets.
And I'm walking to the school.
And as I come to the school, my teacher's already outside and saying, there's no class today.
Go home.
And go home meant, I'm going home to my mother.
That's the way I understood it.
And as I come home and I knock on the door not to frighten her, and she opens the door.
She is screeching and screaming out of the top of her lungs.
What are you doing here?
What are you doing walking the streets?
You're supposed to be at home.
I said, I don't know.
Where's dad?
Dad is in hiding, never mind.
Go back with the sub.
We will call you when everything is all right.
So I went down to the metro, not looking left and right.
I mean, at this time, I was already not feeling too well, because on my march to the school, I went through the Jewish district, [GERMAN], where the Polish Jews lived.
And they had their little kosher meat markets and their bakery goods and all that and the little [GERMAN] and all that.
And I was standing in front of a [GERMAN] while one of the big Brownshirts was carrying out a Torah.
He was carrying out all kinds of implements, putting them in street, lighting a match to it.
And [INAUDIBLE], talks in German to me, isn't that wonderful?
I mean, can you see it?
We finally got them!
I mean, we can destroy it.
Look at this burning.
Here, have a match and try, too.
And the more he talked, the more scared I got.
I mean, I was just a little guy, never mind he was a burly man.
I said, no, no, I got to go to school.
In my best Berlin-dialect German, I go to school.
I can't stay with you.
And so I wasn't feeling too well.
Up in the metro, I went home.
To my home, boys' home, I went.
And there, the people, the husband and wife, Mr.
And Mrs.
Kapelna, explained to me what had happened the forgone night.
And then, of course, later on in my lifetime, I read about it.
I studied about it.
I knew about it.
But at that time, all it was mean, persecution.
That's all I knew about it.
Going to Dachau, going to Buchenwald and all this, this is all later on that meant anything to me.
And after about two weeks of time, they called me.
My father called me and says, it's all right to go back to school.
So I went back to school.
Half of the class was not there.
The teacher explained that people tried to get out of Germany because bad times are there.
And we'll try the best we can, and so on, so forth.
On the 27th of November, 1938, I went to school in the morning.
And the teacher said, go on home.
Your father wants you at home.
Excuse me.
And I arrive at home.
My father's already there.
And in his typical fashion, says, don't ask questions, none whatsoever.
I will explain everything later.
By about 11 o'clock in the morning, we went shopping.
He shopped for suits, underwear, new shoes, everything.
It was a joy.
I had a ball with him, not knowing why.
In the afternoon, we came back home.
Each had a small attache case.
And he said-- I can hear him saying to my mother, so are you coming with us?
Are you not coming with us?
And she says, no.
I can't leave my furniture.
I can't leave my house.
I can't leave my money.
And-- are we stopping for a moment?
OK.
